mnemonics.title: Fork Return Values
mnemonics.content: PARENT gets Positive PID (like P for Parent = Positive), CHILD gets Zero (C for Child = Zero), FAILURE gets Negative (F for Failure = -1)

mnemonics.title: Zombie Prevention
mnemonics.content: Remember: Parent MUST WAIT to prevent ZOMBIES. W-A-I-T = Wait And Intercept Termination. No wait() = zombie created.

mnemonics.title: Orphan Process
mnemonics.content: ORPHAN = Parent Abandoned. When PARENT dies, CHILD becomes INIT's ward. Think: Orphaned children go to the orphanage (init=1).

acronyms.usage: Memory optimization in fork() where pages are shared until modified
Unique number assigned to each process by the kernel
PID of the parent process that created this process
System call that creates a new process by duplicating the existing process

visualTips.visualization: Draw a decision diamond: fork() called → YES: returns 0 to child, returns PID to parent | NO: returns -1 to parent. Child goes LEFT, Parent goes RIGHT from the fork point.
Tree diagram: Root = init (PID 1), branches = parent processes, leaves = child processes. Show PPID pointing upward.
Two scenarios: 1) Zombie: Child box with X, Parent box alive but not calling wait(). 2) Orphan: Child box alive, Parent box with X, arrow to init.

analogies.analogy: fork() is like cell mitosis - one cell (parent) divides into two identical cells (parent + child), each with same DNA (code/data) but separate existence.
Instead of copying the entire book immediately, you share one book (read-only). Only when someone wants to write notes (modify) do they get their own copy.
When someone dies (process terminates), their inheritance (process table entry) waits for someone to claim it (wait()). Unclaimed = zombie.
An orphan process is like a child whose parents died - the government (init process, PID 1) takes responsibility.
